Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd (In Liquidation)

Facts.Text: Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore on 13 October 1995.
By 2007, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was involved in three lines of business: outer port limit offshore supply vessel services around Singapore, ship management, and ship chartering.

From the financial year ending 31 December 2002 onwards, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd showed a negative equity position on its balance sheet, which increased in magnitude over subsequent years up to at least 31 March 2011.
In August 2007, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd obtained a loan of US$1,000,000 from Parakou Shipping SA to finance construction of new outer port limit vessels.
In September 2007, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd entered into a shipbuilding contract of about S$6,000,000 for delivery of outer port limit vessels from August 2008 to January 2009.
In September 2007, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd obtained a loan of S$6,120,000 from Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.
In February 2008, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd obtained a loan of S$1,000,000 from Parakou Shipping SA to finance construction of new outer port limit vessels.

A document titled 'Minutes of a meeting of the Board of Directors' dated 17 March 2008 and signed by Liu Cheng Chan as chairman recorded that for the purpose of restructuring, Liu Cheng Chan and Chik Sau Kam would transfer all their shares in Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Liu Por and Yang Jianguo within 2008, that the company would terminate operation of outer port limit boats and ship management within 2008, and that the company would sell all of its supply boats within 2008.

On 10 June 2008, senior manager Du Hong reported internally that Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd had, with Yang Jianguo's authority, entered into a new five-year fixture with Galsworthy Limited as disponent owners for the vessel later known as Canton Trader, with an intended back-to-back sub-charter to Ocean Glory Shipping Ltd.

On 17 June 2008, Clarkson Asia Limited sent Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd a 'Clean Recap' for the vessel Canton Trader to conclude a fixture between Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and Galsworthy Limited for a minimum five-year charter commencing around March or April 2009.

On 17 June 2008, Ocean Glory Shipping Ltd sent Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd a 'Clean Recap' for the vessel Canton Trader to conclude a sub-charter fixture between Ocean Glory Shipping Ltd and Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd on substantially back-to-back terms with the head charter.
On 17 July 2008, Clarkson Asia Limited sent Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd a copy of the Canton Trader charterparty signed by Galsworthy Limited.
In July 2007, Du Hong joined Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd as a senior manager responsible for the chartering business and reported to Yang Jianguo.
On 20 May 2008, the Baltic Dry Index reached a peak of 11,793 points, before declining sharply later in 2008, affecting dry bulk freight rates Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd's chartering business.
By the end of September 2008, the Baltic Dry Index had fallen to about 3,000 points from its May 2008 peak, indicating a collapse in the freight market.
By the end of October 2008, the Baltic Dry Index had fallen further to about 770 points, a historical low, indicating severe deterioration in the dry bulk freight market.

On 30 October 2008, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd received an email warning it to pay close attention to the financial condition of Ocean Glory Shipping Ltd because Ocean Glory had redelivered a vessel chartered from Cosco (H.K.) earlier than the minimum period with very short notice.

On 31 October 2008, Clarkson Asia Limited sent Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd the original charterparty documents for the Canton Trader, and Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd replied that the file could not be found and queried whether it was the correct party, to which Clarkson confirmed that Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was the charterer.

On 14 November 2008, as directors of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Liu Cheng Chan and Chik Sau Kam passed a directors' resolution authorising Liu Por and Yang Jianguo to execute documents for the sale of ten vessels and two hulls used in the outer port limit business.
On 17 November 2008, Liu Por and Yang Jianguo signed documents on behalf of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd for the sale of ten outer port limit vessels and two hulls to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 17 November 2008, as directors of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, Liu Cheng Chan and Chik Sau Kam signed a directors' resolution, prepared by Liu Por and Yang Jianguo, resolving that Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd would purchase the outer port limit vessels from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and authorising Liu Por to act on behalf of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 18 November 2008, Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore to conduct shipping lines and freight business, including taking over ship management and outer port limit operations previously carried on by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.
With effect from 30 November 2008, twelve ship management agreements between Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and twelve 'Pretty' entities for management of twelve vessels were terminated.

With effect from 1 December 2008, the twelve 'Pretty' entities entered into new ship management agreements with Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd on substantially the same terms as their previous agreements with Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.

Each 'Pretty' entity was wholly owned by Parakou International Limited, whose shareholders included Liu Cheng Chan, Chik Sau Kam, Lau Hoi, and Liu Por, and whose directors for the Pretty entities included Liu Cheng Chan, Chik Sau Kam, and Lau Hoi.

On 22 December 2008, Liu Cheng Chan and Chik Sau Kam transferred their shares in Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Liu Por and Yang Jianguo, appointed Liu Por and Yang Jianguo as directors of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, and gave notice of their resignation as directors with effect from 31 December 2008.

Between 12 November 2008 and 24 November 2008, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd repaid a total of S$9,812,542.80 to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd in respect of amounts owing, with approvals given by Liu Por and Yang Jianguo.

On 9 December 2008, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd set off part of the amount of S$1,732,239.17 owed by Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd against amounts owed by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, with approval by Liu Por and Yang Jianguo.

On 15 December 2008, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd completed a further set-off so that a total of S$1,732,239.17 owing from Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd was set off against amounts owed by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, with approval by Liu Por and Yang Jianguo.

On 5 December 2008, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd repaid S$3,046,200 to Parakou Shipping SA in respect of outstanding loans, following a payment request signed by Liu Cheng Chan and authorisation by Yang Jianguo.

On 23 December 2008, as directors of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Liu Por and Yang Jianguo signed a directors' resolution noting that the ship management agreements with the 'Pretty' entities had been terminated with effect from 30 November 2008 and that 39 employees would be relieved of their duties with effect from 31 December 2008.
With effect from 31 December 2008, the employment of 39 identified employees of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was terminated in accordance with the 23 December 2008 directors' resolution.
In January 2009, the 39 employees whose employment with Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd had ended on 31 December 2008 were employed by Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd.
Between January 2009 and December 2010, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid salaries totalling S$309,376.85 for six employees who were then employed by Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd.
On 12 December 2008, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid bonuses totalling S$267,127.50 to Liu Cheng Chan (S$100,000), Chik Sau Kam (S$80,000), Liu Por (S$39,000), and Yang Jianguo (S$48,127.50).
With effect from 1 January 2009, the monthly salaries of Liu Por and Yang Jianguo were increased, resulting in additional salary payments totalling S$108,000 between January 2009 and March 2011.

Between January 2009 and December 2010, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid a total of S$240,000 as rent to or on behalf of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd for office space where Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd occupied part of the premises.
On 26 January 2009, Clarkson Asia Limited sent Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd a reminder requesting the executed charterparty for the Canton Trader, which was to be renamed Jin Kang.
On 11 February 2009, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd informed Clarkson Asia Limited that it would not execute the Canton Trader charterparty and stated that there was no fixture.
In February 2009, Galsworthy Limited commenced arbitration proceedings in London against Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd under the Canton Trader charterparty.

On 27 June 2009, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd commenced proceedings in Hong Kong against the owners and/or demise charterers of the vessel Jin Kang (formerly Canton Trader) seeking damages and/or an indemnity in respect of any liability in the London arbitration.
On 31 August 2010, an arbitral award in the London proceedings ordered Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to pay Galsworthy Limited US$2,673,279.15 with further damages to be assessed.
On 22 September 2010, the Hong Kong court struck out Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd's claim in the Hong Kong proceedings as a collateral attack on the London arbitration outcome.
On 18 March 2011, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was placed under provisional liquidation.
On 14 April 2011, following a creditors' meeting, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was placed under creditors' voluntary liquidation.
On 13 May 2011, a second arbitral award in the London proceedings ordered Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to pay Galsworthy Limited further damages of US$38,579,000.
Between February 2009 and April 2011, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd incurred legal fees totalling approximately S$6,223,238.72 in relation to the London arbitration and the Hong Kong proceedings.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether various pre-liquidation transactions and payments involving Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and related parties, including sale of vessels, transfer of ship management business, repayments and set-offs of related-party debts, bonuses, salary increases, salary payments for employees of another company, and rent payments, as well as decisions to enter into and respond to charterparty arrangements, are associated with the final outcome of the plaintiff's claims against each defendant.
Party_Details.Rule: In company and insolvency law, directors and persons effectively directing a company are generally manage the company in accordance with its constitutional documents and applicable statutes, and when the company is insolvent or in serious financial difficulty, to take into account the interests of creditors when making decisions about asset dispositions, related-party transactions, and litigation conduct. Statutory avoidance regimes may permit transactions at an undervalue or preferential transactions within defined look-back periods to be unwound where specified conditions, including insolvency and value disparity, are met.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute conduct of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and related entities shows a pattern of intra-group transactions and structural changes during a period of deteriorating freight markets and negative equity. These include the June 2008 Canton Trader fixture arrangements with Galsworthy Limited and Ocean Glory Shipping Ltd, the November 2008 authorisation and execution of a sale of outer port limit vessels to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd based on a contemporaneous valuation, the November and December 2008 repayments and set-offs of substantial sums between Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and Parakou Shipping SA, the November–December 2008 termination and re-entry of ship management agreements from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, the December 2008 transfer of shareholdings and board control from Liu Cheng Chan and Chik Sau Kam to Liu Por and Yang Jianguo, the termination and rehiring of 39 employees from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, the payment of bonuses and subsequent salary increases to key individuals, the continued payment of salaries for six employees employed by Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, and the payment of rent for premises tenanted by Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd while Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd occupied part of the space. These events occurred against a background of a collapsing Baltic Dry Index, negative equity, and the emergence and pursuit of substantial claims under the Canton Trader charterparty, culminating in arbitral awards and eventual liquidation. The relationship between these factual patterns and the final judicial outcome turns on how the court characterised the nature and value of the transactions, the roles of the individuals involved, and the timing of the company’s financial distress relative to the impugned conduct.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court analysed whether certain individuals were shadow or de facto directors, applying section 4 of the Companies Act and authorities such as Raffles Town Club Pte Ltd v Lim Eng Hock Peter and others and Secretary of State for Trade and Industry v Deverell and another. It concluded that Liu Cheng Chan remained a shadow director after 31 December 2008 based on continued influence and consultation, but that there was insufficient evidence that Chik Sau Kam was a shadow director. The court held that Liu Por and Yang Jianguo were not de facto directors before 22 December 2008, finding their roles as President and Vice-President and their conduct consistent with senior management rather than board-level governance.

On the alleged restructuring plan, the court scrutinised the March 2008 Resolution and found it was not contemporaneous, citing inconsistencies in pleadings about attendance, the unusual drafting by the accounts manager rather than the company secretary or lawyers, the absence of references to key elements such as PIH and PSMPL, and contradictions with contemporaneous expansion of the outer port limit business. The court noted non-disclosure of the resolution to auditors and in South African proceedings, and statements that the share transfer agreement had not been reduced to writing. It concluded there was no genuine restructuring plan and that the resolution was an afterthought.

Regarding solvency, the court applied both balance sheet and cash flow tests under section 100(4) of the Bankruptcy Act and related case law such as Chip Thye Enterprises Pte Ltd (in liquidation) v Phay Gi Mo and others, Progen, and Dynasty Line Ltd (in liquidation) v Sukamto Sia. It found Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was balance sheet insolvent throughout the material period, rejecting an approach that notionally removed related-party debts based on letters of support, and noting that such an approach had been criticised in Kon Yin Tong and another v Leow Boon Cher and others. For cash flow, the court accepted that trade creditors were being paid but held that the substantial contingent liability to Galsworthy Limited under the Canton Trader charterparty had to be taken into account under section 254(2)(c) of the Companies Act and Kon Yin Tong. It found that a binding charterparty existed, relying on contemporaneous emails, clean recaps, the signed charterparty, and subsequent conduct, and that legal advice from counsel indicated that Parakou had no viable defence. The court held that the likelihood of the Galsworthy claim materialising was high and that letters of support from related parties were not honoured in practice, as shown by demands for repayment and repayments to PIH and PSSA, so they could not be relied on to establish cash flow solvency. It concluded that Parakou was cash flow insolvent and, in any event, in a parlous financial position requiring directors to consider creditors' interests.

On statutory undervalue transactions under section 329 of the Companies Act read with sections 98 and 100 of the Bankruptcy Act, the court accepted that the impugned dealings were 'transactions' within the five-year relevant time and that insolvency was satisfied, including via the associate presumption. It then assessed whether the transactions were at an undervalue, applying the standard that value is assessed in commercial context and that a significant disparity is required, with reference to authorities such as Mercator & Noordstar NV v Velstra Pte Ltd (in liquidation), Show Theatres Pte Ltd (in liquidation) v Shaw Theatres Pte Ltd, and Ailyan v Smith.

For the sale of the outer port limit vessels to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, the court considered the Amsbach desktop valuation report and expert evidence from Michael Meade. It noted that the sale price was at the low end of the valuation range, that there was no marketing to third parties, and that the sale was rushed to a related party. The court accepted that an arm's-length sale would likely have occurred around the mid-range of the valuation and quantified a loss of S$1,192,900 by comparing the mid-range aggregate value to the gross sale price. It held that the sale was at an undervalue.

For the transfer of ship management agreements from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, the court treated the coordinated termination and re-entry of agreements as an 'arrangement' constituting a transaction. It found that Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd provided no consideration for the transfer and therefore the transaction was at an undervalue under section 98(3)(a). However, in assessing directors' duties, the court later observed that the ship management business was a cost centre serving related-party vessels and was loss-making, and concluded that the transfer could be consistent with creditors' interests.

Regarding the December 2008 bonus payments, the court noted that they were unprecedented, not contractually required, and paid in a context of insolvency. It held that Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd received no consideration for these bonuses and treated them as transactions at an undervalue. For the salary increases of Liu Por and Yang Jianguo, the court found insufficient evidence to characterise them as undervalue transactions. For the employees' salary payments made by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd for six employees employed by Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, the court relied on the December 2008 directors' resolution listing these employees among those whose employment with Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd ended, rejected the explanation that their inclusion was erroneous, and held that Parakou received no consideration for paying their salaries, treating these payments as undervalue transactions. For the rent payments, the court found the plaintiff's estimate of excess rent speculative and declined to treat them as undervalue transactions.

On directors' duties, the court applied the principle that when a company is insolvent or nearly insolvent, directors must consider creditors' interests, citing Progen and Dynasty. It held that, given Parakou's financial condition and the Galsworthy exposure, the directors were under such a duty. The court found that the rushed sale of the outer port limit vessels to a related party at an undervalue, the substantial repayments to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and Parakou Shipping SA despite letters of support, the unprecedented bonuses and salary increases, and the payment of salaries for employees of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd were not in the interests of creditors and constituted breaches of fiduciary duties by the directors involved. It distinguished the PIH set-off, reasoning that mutual set-off would have been available in any event under insolvency set-off rules and that the set-off did not improve Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd's position in a winding up, so it was neither an undue preference nor contrary to creditors' interests. It also concluded that the transfer of the ship management agreements, given the loss-making nature of the business, was consistent with creditors' interests and not a breach of duty, and that the evidence did not support a finding that the rent payments were contrary to creditors' interests.

The court addressed the interaction between undue preference claims and breach of fiduciary duty claims, referring to Living the Link Pte Ltd (in creditors' voluntary liquidation) and others v Tan Lay Tin Tina and others. It accepted that statutory preference claims and fiduciary duty claims are distinct causes of action and that courts should be cautious about allowing fiduciary duty claims to circumvent statutory look-back limits. However, it held that the mere fact that the PIH and PSSA repayments fell outside the two-year claw-back period did not immunise directors from liability for breach of duty where there was no legitimate reason for the payments and they were clearly not in creditors' interests. It adopted the approach that, where directors are ordered to restore the value of such payments, any dividends attributable to those restored sums should be recouped to the directors to avoid overcompensating unsecured creditors.

On the allegation that the directors breached their duties by defending the London arbitration and pursuing the Hong Kong proceedings to delay liquidation beyond the preference claw-back period, the court examined the timeline of the PIH and PSSA repayments, the two-year period, the conduct of the arbitration and litigation, and the advice received. It noted that the directors were aware of the claw-back period and that legal advice indicated that Parakou's defence in the London arbitration was weak. It also noted steps that could be seen as delaying tactics, such as resisting expedited procedures and resisting disclosure from Clarkson, and the commencement of the Hong Kong proceedings. However, the court also observed that Parakou admitted liability for a substantial sum in the arbitration, leading to an award that could have supported a winding-up application before the expiry of the two-year period, and that the Hong Kong appeal continued even after the two-year period had expired. It accepted evidence that the litigation strategy aimed to exert commercial pressure on Galsworthy by tying up security and positioning for a negotiated settlement, rather than solely to avoid preference claims. The court therefore declined to find that the decisions to defend the arbitration and pursue the Hong Kong proceedings were breaches of duty.

For accessory liability, the court applied the tests for dishonest assistance and knowing receipt from George Raymond Zage III and another v Ho Chi Kwong and another and Bank of Credit and Commerce International (Overseas) Ltd and another v Akindele, and the attribution principles in Bilta (UK) Ltd (in liquidation) and others v Nazir and others (No 2). It held that the directors of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd were in a trustee-like position regarding company assets. It found that Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, whose directors included Liu Cheng Chan and Chik Sau Kam, assisted in and benefited from the sale of the outer port limit vessels and the PIH repayments, and that the knowledge of its directors about the breaches of duty was attributable to the company. It concluded that Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd was liable for dishonest assistance and knowing receipt in relation to those transactions, and that any dividends attributable to restored sums should be recouped to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd. For Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, whose directors included Liu Cheng Chan, Chik Sau Kam, Liu Por, and Yang Jianguo, the court found that it assisted in the breaches relating to the employees' salary payments by allowing Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to pay salaries for its employees, and that the directors' knowledge was attributable to it. It held Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd liable for dishonest assistance in respect of those payments and held the relevant directors jointly and severally liable with it.

The court considered but did not accept broader veil-piercing arguments beyond the accessory liability findings, emphasising that piercing the corporate veil is exceptional and that the facts did not justify treating the companies' receipts as those of the individuals beyond the established dishonest assistance and knowing receipt frameworks. It also addressed and rejected certain conspiracy and unlawful means claims where the evidential threshold was not met. Ultimately, the court found that the plaintiff had established liability for breaches of directors' duties, dishonest assistance, knowing receipt, and statutory undervalue transactions in respect of specific impugned dealings, and it granted remedies including restoration of value and related relief, while dismissing other heads of claim where the legal or evidential requirements were not satisfied.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Liu Cheng Chan

Facts.Text: From the time of incorporation of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd on 13 October 1995, Liu Cheng Chan was a shareholder and director of the company.
On incorporation of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Liu Cheng Chan held 50% of the shares in the company.
With effect from 1 January 2005, following a change in shareholdings, Liu Cheng Chan's shareholding in Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd increased to 80%.
After 21 December 2008, Liu Cheng Chan ceased to be a shareholder of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.
With effect from 31 December 2008, Liu Cheng Chan ceased to be a director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.
From 8 January 1997, Liu Cheng Chan was a director and shareholder of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, holding 70% of its shares during the material period.
From 18 November 2008, Liu Cheng Chan was a director and shareholder of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, holding 50% of its shares during the material period.
During the material period, Liu Cheng Chan was the sole shareholder of Parakou Shipping SA and a director of that company.

On 17 March 2008, a document titled 'Minutes of a meeting of the Board of Directors' of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was signed by Liu Cheng Chan as chairman, recording proposed transfer of his and Chik Sau Kam's shares to Liu Por and Yang Jianguo within 2008, termination of outer port limit and ship management operations within 2008, and sale of all supply boats within 2008.

On 14 November 2008, as a director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Liu Cheng Chan signed a directors' resolution authorising Liu Por and Yang Jianguo to execute documents for the sale of ten outer port limit vessels and two hulls.

On 17 November 2008, as a director of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, Liu Cheng Chan signed a directors' resolution resolving that Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd would purchase outer port limit vessels from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and authorising Liu Por to act on behalf of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

In August 2007, as sole shareholder of Parakou Shipping SA, Liu Cheng Chan caused Parakou Shipping SA to extend a loan of US$1,000,000 to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd for construction of new outer port limit vessels.

In February 2008, as sole shareholder of Parakou Shipping SA, Liu Cheng Chan caused Parakou Shipping SA to extend a loan of S$1,000,000 to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd for construction of new outer port limit vessels.

On 7 July 2008, Liu Cheng Chan signed a letter of financial support addressed to the auditors of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd stating his intention to continue to provide adequate finance to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd until all other payables had been met.

On 7 July 2008, as representative of Parakou Shipping SA, Liu Cheng Chan signed a letter of financial support to the auditors of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd stating that Parakou Shipping SA intended to continue to provide adequate finance to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd until all other payables had been met.
On 20 November 2008, acting for Parakou Shipping SA, Liu Cheng Chan issued a demand letter requesting repayment of all outstanding loans from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Shipping SA.

On 5 December 2008, a payment request from Parakou Shipping SA to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd for S$3,046,200 was signed by Liu Cheng Chan, leading to repayment of that amount by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.
On 12 December 2008, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid a bonus of S$100,000 to Liu Cheng Chan.

On 28 March 2009, in an affidavit filed in South African proceedings concerning the arrest of the vessel Pretty Time, Liu Cheng Chan stated that a 2008 decision to transfer shares in Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Liu Por and Yang Jianguo had not been reduced to writing.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute conduct and corporate roles of Liu Cheng Chan in relation to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and related entities, including authorising asset sales, directing related-party loans and repayments, and participating in structural changes, are associated with the final outcome of the claims against him.
Party_Details.Rule: Individuals who act as directors or in equivalent positions of control over a company are generally manage the company in accordance with its constitution and applicable law, and when the company is insolvent or in serious financial difficulty, to take into account the interests of creditors when deciding on asset dispositions, related-party transactions, and other significant corporate actions.
Party_Details.Application: The factual record shows that Liu Cheng Chan held controlling shareholdings and board positions in Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, Parakou Shipping SA, and Parakou International Limited during the relevant period. He signed a March 2008 board document recording proposed transfer of his shares and changes to business lines, authorised the November 2008 sale of outer port limit vessels from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, and as a director of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd approved that company’s purchase of those vessels. He also controlled loans from Parakou Shipping SA to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, issued a demand for repayment in November 2008, and signed a payment request that led to repayment of S$3,046,200. He received a substantial bonus in December 2008. These actions occurred while Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd had negative equity and was exposed to a large contingent claim under the Canton Trader charterparty. The court’s final assessment of his liability turned on how it characterised these transactions and his role in them under the applicable legal standards.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court treated Liu Cheng Chan as a central decision-maker in the Parakou group. It found, based on his own evidence and contemporaneous communications, that he continued to be consulted on significant issues and to receive operational updates even after resigning as a formal director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, and that he influenced decisions regarding the Galsworthy dispute, including instructing that lawyers be appointed. Applying the statutory definition of 'director' and case law on shadow directorship, the court concluded that he remained a shadow director after 31 December 2008 and was subject to the same fiduciary obligations as an appointed director.

The court examined the March 2008 board document signed by him and concluded that it was not contemporaneous, citing inconsistencies in pleadings about attendance, the unusual drafting by the accounts manager, non-disclosure to auditors and foreign courts, and contradictions with the company’s expansion of the outer port limit business. It held that the purported restructuring plan recorded in that document was an after-the-fact justification for later transactions.

In assessing solvency and directors' duties, the court found that Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was balance sheet and cash flow insolvent during the material period and that directors, including shadow directors, were required to consider creditors' interests. It then evaluated specific transactions authorised or influenced by Liu Cheng Chan. For the sale of the outer port limit vessels to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, the court relied on valuation evidence and expert testimony to find that the sale at the low end of the valuation range, without marketing and in a rushed intra-group context, resulted in a significant shortfall compared to a mid-range arm's-length value. It held that this sale was at an undervalue and not in creditors' interests.

For the repayments to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and Parakou Shipping SA, the court noted that these were made shortly after letters of financial support had been given and while Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was insolvent and facing a substantial contingent claim. It concluded that the repayments had the effect of preferring related creditors and were not in the interests of other creditors. Although the statutory look-back period for undue preference claims had expired, the court held that this did not prevent a finding that a director who procured such payments had breached fiduciary duties, and it adopted a recoupment mechanism for dividends attributable to any restored sums.

The court also considered the December 2008 bonus payments, including the S$100,000 paid to Liu Cheng Chan, and found that they were unprecedented, not contractually required, and made in an insolvency context. It held that the company received no consideration for these bonuses and that authorising or accepting them was inconsistent with the duty to consider creditors' interests. For the employees' salary payments made by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd for employees of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, the court found that the directors' resolution showed that these employees had left Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, rejected the explanation that their inclusion was erroneous, and concluded that causing Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to bear these costs was not in creditors' interests.

On accessory liability, the court attributed Liu Cheng Chan’s knowledge and conduct to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd for the purposes of dishonest assistance and knowing receipt. It held that Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, through its directors including him, assisted in and benefited from the undervalue sale of the outer port limit vessels and the repayments, and that its knowledge of the circumstances made it unconscionable to retain those benefits. It similarly held that Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, through its directors including him, assisted in the employees' salary payments. The court therefore found him liable, together with other relevant individuals and companies, under the heads of breach of directors' duties and accessory liability in relation to the specified transactions, while rejecting other allegations where the evidential threshold was not met.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Chik Sau Kam

Facts.Text: From the time of incorporation of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd on 13 October 1995, Chik Sau Kam was a shareholder and director of the company.
On incorporation of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Chik Sau Kam held 50% of the shares in the company.
In 2005, following a change in shareholdings, Chik Sau Kam's shareholding in Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was reduced to 8.33%.
After 21 December 2008, Chik Sau Kam ceased to be a shareholder of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.
With effect from 31 December 2008, Chik Sau Kam ceased to be a director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.
From 8 January 1997, Chik Sau Kam was a director and shareholder of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd during the material period.
From 18 November 2008, Chik Sau Kam was a director and shareholder of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, holding 30% of its shares during the material period.

During the material period, Chik Sau Kam was a director of the 'Pretty' entities that owned the 'Pretty' vessels managed under ship management agreements with Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and later Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd.

On 17 March 2008, Chik Sau Kam signed the attendance sheet for a document titled 'Minutes of a meeting of the Board of Directors' of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd that recorded proposed transfer of her and Liu Cheng Chan's shares to Liu Por and Yang Jianguo within 2008, termination of outer port limit and ship management operations within 2008, and sale of all supply boats within 2008.

On 14 November 2008, as a director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Chik Sau Kam participated in passing a directors' resolution authorising Liu Por and Yang Jianguo to execute documents for the sale of ten outer port limit vessels and two hulls.

On 17 November 2008, as a director of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, Chik Sau Kam signed a directors' resolution resolving that Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd would purchase outer port limit vessels from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and authorising Liu Por to act on behalf of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 22 December 2008, as a shareholder and director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Chik Sau Kam transferred her shares in Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Liu Por and Yang Jianguo, appointed them as directors, and gave notice of her resignation as director with effect from 31 December 2008.

In September 2007, as a shareholder and director of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, Chik Sau Kam was involved in Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd extending a loan of S$6,120,000 to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.

On 7 July 2008, as a representative of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, Chik Sau Kam signed a letter of financial support to the auditors of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd stating that Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd intended to continue to provide adequate finance to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd until all other payables had been met.
On 12 December 2008, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid a bonus of S$80,000 to Chik Sau Kam.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute conduct and corporate roles of Chik Sau Kam in relation to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and related entities, including participation in asset sales, related-party financing, and share and board changes, are associated with the final outcome of the claims against her.
Party_Details.Rule: Individuals who serve as directors or exercise control over a company are generally manage the company in accordance with its constitution and applicable law, and when the company is insolvent or in serious financial difficulty, to take into account the interests of creditors when approving asset dispositions, related-party transactions, and other significant corporate actions.
Party_Details.Application: The factual record shows that Chik Sau Kam was a co-founder, shareholder, and director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, and also held directorships and shareholdings in Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, and the 'Pretty' entities. She signed the March 2008 board document recording proposed changes to shareholding and business focus, participated in the November 2008 resolution authorising the sale of outer port limit vessels from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, and as a director of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd approved that company's purchase of those vessels. She was involved in related-party financing arrangements and signed a letter of financial support on behalf of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, and she received a substantial bonus in December 2008. These actions occurred while Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd had negative equity and was exposed to a large contingent claim under the Canton Trader charterparty. The court's final assessment of her liability turned on how it characterised her role in the impugned transactions and the extent of her ongoing involvement after her resignation date.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court recognised that Chik Sau Kam was a long-standing shareholder and director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and related entities, but it distinguished her level of operational involvement from that of Liu Cheng Chan. It accepted her evidence that she generally followed the advice of others and did not play an active role in day-to-day management. On the question of shadow directorship after 31 December 2008, the court found insufficient evidence that she continued to direct or influence the board's decisions and therefore declined to treat her as a shadow director beyond her formal resignation.

However, for the period during which she was an appointed director, the court examined her participation in key resolutions and transactions. It noted that she signed the attendance sheet for the March 2008 board document and co-signed the November 2008 resolution authorising the sale of outer port limit vessels from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, as well as the Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd resolution approving the purchase of those vessels. It also noted her role in related-party financing, including the Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd loan and the letter of financial support, and her receipt of a significant bonus in December 2008.

Applying the insolvency and directors' duties analysis, the court concluded that by late 2008 Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was insolvent or in a parlous financial state, and that directors were required to consider creditors' interests. It held that the sale of the outer port limit vessels to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd at a value below a reasonable arm's-length level, the substantial repayments to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and Parakou Shipping SA, and the unprecedented bonuses were not in creditors' interests. It found that, as a director at the time these decisions were made and implemented, she shared responsibility for authorising or permitting these transactions. While the statutory look-back period for undue preference claims had expired, the court held that this did not prevent a finding that a director who had approved such payments had breached fiduciary duties.

In relation to accessory liability, the court attributed the knowledge and actions of the directors of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, including Chik Sau Kam, to that company. It held that Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd was liable for dishonest assistance and knowing receipt in respect of the undervalue sale and repayments, and that the directors who controlled it, including her, were responsible for its participation in those transactions. The court therefore found her liable under the heads of breach of directors' duties and, through her role in Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, for the company's accessory liability, while limiting her post-resignation exposure by declining to treat her as a shadow director.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Liu Por

Facts.Text: With effect from 1 January 2005, Liu Por became a shareholder of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and held 11.67% of its shares at that time.
From 2005, Liu Por was employed by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.
From 2006, Liu Por held a general management role as Vice-President of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and oversaw its day-to-day operations.
On 22 December 2008, Liu Por was appointed as a director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.
From 22 December 2008, Liu Por held 70% of the shares in Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.
During the material period, Liu Por was a director and shareholder of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.
From 18 November 2008, Liu Por was a director and shareholder of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, holding 20% of its shares.
On 14 November 2008, as Vice-President of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Liu Por was authorised by a directors' resolution to execute documents for the sale of ten outer port limit vessels and two hulls.
On 17 November 2008, acting for Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Liu Por signed documents for the sale of ten outer port limit vessels and two hulls to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 17 November 2008, as a director of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, Liu Por was authorised by a directors' resolution to act on behalf of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd in purchasing the outer port limit vessels from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.

Between 12 November 2008 and 24 November 2008, as a senior manager of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Liu Por approved repayments totalling S$9,812,542.80 from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 9 December 2008, as a senior manager of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Liu Por approved a set-off of part of S$1,732,239.17 owed by Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd against amounts owed by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 15 December 2008, as a senior manager of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Liu Por approved completion of the set-off so that a total of S$1,732,239.17 owed by Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd was set off against amounts owed by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 23 December 2008, as a director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Liu Por signed a directors' resolution noting termination of ship management agreements with the 'Pretty' entities and resolving that 39 employees would be relieved of their duties with effect from 31 December 2008.
On 12 December 2008, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid a bonus of S$39,000 to Liu Por.

With effect from 1 January 2009, the salary of Liu Por at Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was increased, resulting in additional salary payments totalling S$108,000 between January 2009 and March 2011 shared with Yang Jianguo.

Between January 2009 and December 2010, while serving as a director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, Liu Por was involved in arrangements under which Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid salaries totalling S$309,376.85 for six employees employed by Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd.

Between January 2009 and December 2010, while serving as a director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, Liu Por was involved in arrangements under which Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid S$240,000 as rent to or on behalf of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd for office space.

On 19 November 2008, through internal communications relayed by accounts manager Andy Ng to external lawyers, Liu Por indicated an intention that proceeds from selling the ship management business, outer port limit business, and outer port limit vessels would be used to discharge existing director loans, including a loan owed to Liu Cheng Chan.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute conduct and corporate roles of Liu Por in relation to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and related entities, including executing and approving intra-group asset transfers, repayments, set-offs, employee transfers, and remuneration changes, are associated with the final outcome of the claims against him.
Party_Details.Rule: Individuals who serve as directors or exercise effective control over a company are generally manage the company in accordance with its constitution and applicable law, and when the company is insolvent or in serious financial difficulty, to take into account the interests of creditors when approving asset dispositions, related-party transactions, remuneration decisions, and other significant corporate actions.
Party_Details.Application: The factual record shows that Liu Por progressed from shareholder and Vice-President overseeing day-to-day operations of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to a director and majority shareholder by late 2008, while also holding directorships and shareholdings in Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd. He executed the November 2008 sale of outer port limit vessels from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, approved substantial repayments and set-offs between those companies, and participated in the December 2008 resolution terminating ship management agreements and 39 employees. He received a bonus and salary increases and was involved in arrangements under which Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid salaries for employees of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd and rent for premises tenanted by Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd. These actions occurred while Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd had negative equity and was exposed to a large contingent claim under the Canton Trader charterparty. The court’s final assessment of his liability turned on how it characterised these transactions and his decision-making role under the applicable legal standards.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court considered whether Liu Por was a de facto director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd before his formal appointment on 22 December 2008. It acknowledged his roles as Vice-President and general manager responsible for day-to-day operations and his involvement in significant matters such as the sale of outer port limit vessels and approval of repayments and set-offs. However, applying the criteria for de facto directorship, it concluded that his actions were within the scope of senior management responsibilities and that there was insufficient evidence that he directed the company’s affairs on an equal footing with the board before his appointment. It therefore declined to treat him as a de facto director prior to 22 December 2008.

After his appointment as a director, the court treated him as part of the directing mind of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd. It found that he executed the sale of the outer port limit vessels to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd at a price below a reasonable arm's-length value, based on the valuation evidence and expert testimony, and that he approved substantial repayments and set-offs between Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd. It also found that he co-signed the December 2008 resolution terminating ship management agreements and 39 employees, and that he was involved in subsequent arrangements under which those employees were rehired by Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd while Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd continued to pay salaries for six of them.

In the context of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd's insolvency and exposure to the Galsworthy claim, the court held that directors, including Liu Por, were required to consider creditors' interests. It concluded that the sale of the outer port limit vessels at an undervalue, the repayments to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, the unprecedented bonuses and salary increases, and the payment of salaries for employees of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd were not in creditors' interests and constituted breaches of fiduciary duties by the directors involved. It distinguished the PIH set-off, reasoning that statutory insolvency set-off would have been available and that the set-off did not improve Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd's position in a winding up, and therefore did not treat that set-off as a breach of duty.

The court also examined allegations that he used the London arbitration and Hong Kong proceedings to delay liquidation beyond the preference claw-back period. It noted that he, together with Yang Jianguo, admitted liability for a substantial sum in the arbitration, leading to an award that could have supported a winding-up application before the expiry of the two-year period, and that the Hong Kong appeal continued after that period. It accepted evidence that the litigation strategy aimed to exert settlement pressure on Galsworthy rather than solely to avoid preference claims, and therefore declined to find that his decisions regarding the conduct of those proceedings were breaches of duty.

For accessory liability, the court attributed his knowledge and actions to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd. It held that Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, through its directors including him, was liable for dishonest assistance and knowing receipt in relation to the undervalue sale and repayments, and that Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, through its directors including him, was liable for dishonest assistance in relation to the employees' salary payments. It held him jointly and severally liable with those companies and other relevant individuals for the losses associated with the impugned transactions, while rejecting other claims where the evidential or legal thresholds were not met.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Yang Jianguo

Facts.Text: From 2006, Yang Jianguo was appointed President of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and oversaw its day-to-day operations together with Liu Por.
On 22 December 2008, Yang Jianguo was appointed as a director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and became a 30% shareholder of the company.
From 18 November 2008, Yang Jianguo was a director of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd.
On 14 November 2008, as President of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Yang Jianguo was authorised by a directors' resolution to execute documents for the sale of ten outer port limit vessels and two hulls.
On 17 November 2008, acting for Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Yang Jianguo signed documents for the sale of ten outer port limit vessels and two hulls to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

Between 12 November 2008 and 24 November 2008, as a senior manager of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Yang Jianguo approved repayments totalling S$9,812,542.80 from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 9 December 2008, as a senior manager of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Yang Jianguo approved a set-off of part of S$1,732,239.17 owed by Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd against amounts owed by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 15 December 2008, as a senior manager of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Yang Jianguo approved completion of the set-off so that a total of S$1,732,239.17 owed by Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd was set off against amounts owed by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 5 December 2008, as a senior manager of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Yang Jianguo authorised repayment of S$3,046,200 by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Shipping SA following a payment request signed by Liu Cheng Chan.

On 23 December 2008, as a director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Yang Jianguo signed a directors' resolution noting termination of ship management agreements with the 'Pretty' entities and resolving that 39 employees would be relieved of their duties with effect from 31 December 2008.
On 12 December 2008, Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid a bonus of S$48,127.50 to Yang Jianguo.

With effect from 1 January 2009, the salary of Yang Jianguo at Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was increased, resulting in additional salary payments totalling S$108,000 between January 2009 and March 2011 shared with Liu Por.

Between January 2009 and December 2010, while serving as a director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, Yang Jianguo was involved in arrangements under which Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid salaries totalling S$309,376.85 for six employees employed by Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd.

Between January 2009 and December 2010, while serving as a director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, Yang Jianguo was involved in arrangements under which Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid S$240,000 as rent to or on behalf of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd for office space.

On 10 June 2008, senior manager Du Hong reported internally that Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd had, with Yang Jianguo's authority, entered into a new five-year fixture with Galsworthy Limited for the vessel later known as Canton Trader, with an intended back-to-back sub-charter to Ocean Glory Shipping Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute conduct and corporate roles of Yang Jianguo in relation to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and related entities, including executing and approving intra-group asset transfers, repayments, set-offs, employee transfers, and remuneration changes, as well as authorising charter fixtures, are associated with the final outcome of the claims against him.
Party_Details.Rule: Individuals who serve as directors or exercise effective control over a company are generally manage the company in accordance with its constitution and applicable law, and when the company is insolvent or in serious financial difficulty, to take into account the interests of creditors when approving asset dispositions, related-party transactions, remuneration decisions, and other significant corporate actions.
Party_Details.Application: The factual record shows that Yang Jianguo, as President and later director and shareholder of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, and as a director of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, played a central role in operational and transactional decisions. He authorised the June 2008 Canton Trader fixture, executed the November 2008 sale of outer port limit vessels from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, approved substantial repayments and set-offs between those companies, authorised repayment to Parakou Shipping SA, and co-signed the December 2008 resolution terminating ship management agreements and 39 employees. He received a bonus and salary increases and was involved in arrangements under which Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid salaries for employees of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd and rent for premises tenanted by Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd. These actions occurred while Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd had negative equity and was exposed to a large contingent claim under the Canton Trader charterparty. The court’s final assessment of his liability turned on how it characterised these transactions and his decision-making role under the applicable legal standards.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court considered whether Yang Jianguo was a de facto director of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd before his formal appointment on 22 December 2008. It acknowledged his role as President and his responsibility for day-to-day operations and significant business decisions, including involvement in the sale of outer port limit vessels and approval of repayments and set-offs. However, applying the criteria for de facto directorship, it concluded that his actions were consistent with senior management functions and that there was insufficient evidence that he directed the company's affairs on an equal footing with the board before his appointment. It therefore declined to treat him as a de facto director prior to 22 December 2008.

After his appointment as a director, the court treated him as part of the directing mind of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd. It found that he authorised the Canton Trader fixture, executed the sale of the outer port limit vessels to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd at a value below a reasonable arm's-length level, approved substantial repayments and set-offs between Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, and authorised repayment to Parakou Shipping SA. It also found that he co-signed the December 2008 resolution terminating ship management agreements and 39 employees, and that he was involved in subsequent arrangements under which those employees were rehired by Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd while Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd continued to pay salaries for six of them.

In the context of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd's insolvency and exposure to the Galsworthy claim, the court held that directors, including Yang Jianguo, were required to consider creditors' interests. It concluded that the sale of the outer port limit vessels at an undervalue, the repayments to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd and Parakou Shipping SA, the unprecedented bonuses and salary increases, and the payment of salaries for employees of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd were not in creditors' interests and constituted breaches of fiduciary duties by the directors involved. It distinguished the PIH set-off, reasoning that statutory insolvency set-off would have been available and that the set-off did not improve Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd's position in a winding up, and therefore did not treat that set-off as a breach of duty.

The court also examined allegations that he used the London arbitration and Hong Kong proceedings to delay liquidation beyond the preference claw-back period. It noted that he, together with Liu Por, admitted liability for a substantial sum in the arbitration, leading to an award that could have supported a winding-up application before the expiry of the two-year period, and that the Hong Kong appeal continued after that period. It accepted evidence that the litigation strategy aimed to exert settlement pressure on Galsworthy rather than solely to avoid preference claims, and therefore declined to find that his decisions regarding the conduct of those proceedings were breaches of duty.

For accessory liability, the court attributed his knowledge and actions to Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd. It held that Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, through its directors including him, was liable for dishonest assistance in relation to the employees' salary payments, and that he was jointly and severally liable with that company and other relevant individuals for the losses associated with those payments. It also took his role into account in holding Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd liable for dishonest assistance and knowing receipt in relation to the undervalue sale and repayments, although he was not a shareholder of that company. The court thus found him liable under the heads of breach of directors' duties and accessory liability in relation to specified transactions, while rejecting other claims where the evidential or legal thresholds were not met.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore on 8 January 1997 and carried on general wholesale trade.

From incorporation, the directors and shareholders of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd included Liu Cheng Chan, Chik Sau Kam, and Liu Por, with Liu Cheng Chan holding 70% of its shares during the material period.
In September 2007, Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd extended a loan of S$6,120,000 to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.

On 7 July 2008, Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd issued a letter of financial support to the auditors of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd stating its intention to continue to provide adequate finance to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd until all other payables had been met.

On 17 November 2008, the board of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, including directors Liu Cheng Chan and Chik Sau Kam, passed a resolution to purchase ten outer port limit vessels and two hulls from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and authorised Liu Por to act on behalf of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 17 November 2008, Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd entered into a transaction to purchase ten outer port limit vessels and two hulls from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd for a gross price of S$9,905,600.
Between 12 November 2008 and 24 November 2008, Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd received repayments totalling S$9,812,542.80 from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd in respect of amounts owed.

On 9 December 2008 and 15 December 2008, Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd participated in set-offs totalling S$1,732,239.17, whereby amounts it owed to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd were set off against amounts owed by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd.

Between January 2009 and December 2010, Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd was the tenant of office premises for which Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid S$240,000 in rent while occupying part of the space.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute conduct of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd in entering into asset purchase, lending, repayment, set-off, and rental arrangements with Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd is associated with the final outcome of the claims against it.
Party_Details.Rule: A company that enters into transactions with another company whose assets are managed by fiduciaries may, depending on its knowledge and the circumstances, be exposed to claims such as assistance or knowing receipt if it assists in or benefits from transactions that are later found to be in of those fiduciaries' duties.
Party_Details.Application: The factual record shows that Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, controlled by members of the Liu family, extended a substantial loan to Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, issued a letter of financial support, purchased ten outer port limit vessels and two hulls from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd at a price later compared to valuation ranges, received repayments totalling S$9,812,542.80, participated in set-offs totalling S$1,732,239.17, and was the tenant of premises for which Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid rent while occupying part of the space. These transactions occurred while Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd had negative equity and was exposed to a large contingent claim under the Canton Trader charterparty, and they were approved and implemented by overlapping directors. The court’s final assessment of Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd’s liability turned on how it characterised the value and context of these transactions and the knowledge attributable to the company through its directors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court examined Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd's role in the impugned transactions, focusing on the sale of the outer port limit vessels, the repayments, the set-offs, and the rent payments. It noted that the company was controlled by the same individuals who directed Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and that its board, including Liu Cheng Chan and Chik Sau Kam, resolved to purchase the vessels and authorised Liu Por to act on its behalf.

For the vessel sale, the court relied on the Amsbach valuation and expert evidence to find that the sale price was at the low end of the valuation range and that an arm's-length sale would likely have occurred around the mid-range. It concluded that the sale was at an undervalue and that Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd suffered a quantifiable shortfall. It then considered Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd's knowledge, attributing to it the knowledge of its directors about Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd's financial condition and the nature of the transaction. It held that the company had sufficient knowledge of the circumstances to make its participation in the transaction dishonest for the purposes of dishonest assistance and unconscionable for the purposes of knowing receipt.

For the repayments totalling S$9,812,542.80, the court observed that they were made shortly after Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd had issued a letter of financial support and while Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd was insolvent and facing a substantial contingent claim. It concluded that the repayments were not in the interests of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd's other creditors and that the directors of both companies were aware of this context. It held that Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd, through its directors, assisted in and benefited from these repayments with the requisite knowledge, and therefore was liable for dishonest assistance and knowing receipt. It adopted a recoupment mechanism for dividends attributable to any restored sums to avoid overcompensating unsecured creditors.

For the set-offs totalling S$1,732,239.17, the court reasoned that statutory insolvency set-off would have been available in any event and that the set-offs did not improve Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd's position in a winding up. It therefore did not treat the set-offs as undue preferences or as contrary to creditors' interests and did not base liability on them. For the rent payments, the court found the plaintiff's evidence on excess rent speculative and declined to treat those payments as undervalue transactions or breaches of duty, and accordingly did not impose liability on Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd in respect of them.

Overall, the court concluded that Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd was liable for dishonest assistance and knowing receipt in relation to the undervalue sale of the outer port limit vessels and the repayments from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd, based on the knowledge and actions of its directors, while rejecting liability in relation to the set-offs and rent payments.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore on 18 November 2008 to conduct shipping lines and freight business, including ship management and outer port limit operations.

From incorporation, the directors of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd were Liu Cheng Chan, Chik Sau Kam, Liu Por, and Yang Jianguo, and its shareholders were Liu Cheng Chan (50%), Chik Sau Kam (30%), and Liu Por (20%).
With effect from 30 November 2008, twelve ship management agreements between Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and twelve 'Pretty' entities were terminated.

With effect from 1 December 2008, the twelve 'Pretty' entities entered into new ship management agreements with Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd on substantially the same terms as their previous agreements with Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.
In January 2009, Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd employed 39 individuals who had been employees of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd until 31 December 2008.
Between January 2009 and December 2010, Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd was the employer of six individuals whose salaries totalling S$309,376.85 were paid by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the pre-dispute conduct of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd in receiving ship management business and employees from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and in connection with salary payments made by Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd for its employees is associated with the final outcome of the claims against it.
Party_Details.Rule: A company that receives business, assets, or benefits from another company whose assets are managed by fiduciaries may, depending on its knowledge and the circumstances, be exposed to claims such as assistance or knowing receipt if it assists in or benefits from transactions that are later found to be in of those fiduciaries' duties.
Party_Details.Application: The factual record shows that Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, controlled by members of the Liu family, was incorporated shortly before the termination of ship management agreements between Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and the 'Pretty' entities, and that those entities then entered into substantially similar agreements with Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd. It also shows that 39 employees moved from Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, and that Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd continued to pay salaries for six of these employees while they were employed by Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd. These events occurred while Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd had negative equity and was exposed to a large contingent claim under the Canton Trader charterparty, and they were approved and implemented by overlapping directors. The court’s final assessment of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd’s liability turned on how it characterised the value and context of these transactions and the knowledge attributable to the company through its directors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court examined Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd's role in the transfer of the ship management business and in the salary payments. It accepted that the coordinated termination of ship management agreements between Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd and the 'Pretty' entities and the entry into substantially identical agreements between those entities and Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd constituted an arrangement amounting to a 'transaction' for statutory purposes. It found that Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd provided no consideration for this transfer and therefore treated it as a transaction at an undervalue. However, in assessing directors' duties, the court noted that the ship management business was a cost centre serving related-party vessels and was loss-making, and concluded that transferring this business out of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd could be consistent with creditors' interests. It therefore did not treat the transfer itself as a breach of directors' duties and did not base liability for Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd on knowing receipt in respect of that transfer.

For the employees' salary payments, the court relied on the December 2008 directors' resolution listing the 39 employees, including the six in question, as having their employment with Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd terminated with effect from 31 December 2008. It rejected the explanation that their inclusion was erroneous and found that these six individuals were employees of Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd during the period when Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd paid their salaries. It held that causing Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to bear these salary costs was not in creditors' interests and constituted a breach of fiduciary duties by the directors of Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd.

Applying the tests for dishonest assistance and the attribution principles, the court held that Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd, through its directors including Liu Cheng Chan, Liu Por, and Yang Jianguo, had knowledge of the circumstances and assisted in the breaches by allowing Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd to pay salaries for its employees. It concluded that Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd was liable for dishonest assistance in relation to the employees' salary payments and that the relevant directors were jointly and severally liable with it for the associated losses. The court did not find Parakou Shipmanagement Pte Ltd liable for knowing receipt in relation to the transfer of the ship management agreements, given its conclusion that the transfer was not a breach of directors' duties.
